(22) Learning Hexo from Scratch - SEO: How URLs Affect SEO
This chapter is about SEO. URLs are a topic on their own, with a few small gotchas you'll run into, so let's talk through them.
Before getting into the SEO/URL relationship, let's quickly cover the basics of a URL. I'll use my own blog URL as the example (
The protocol. There are tons of protocols, but you mainly need to know
The third-level domain — also called the subdomain or host. In my case this is my GitHub username, and usually this is whatever you choose.
also lives at this level, which is why Google's home page shows up as

The second-level domain. This is the most important level. When you buy a domain, this is the part you're choosing, and it matters because a well-chosen name boosts SEO — it affects user recognition and how search engines index you. Also called the domain name.
The top-level domain (TLD). This identifies the nature of the site.
originally stood for "company" (early on it was restricted to companies, but that restriction has long since been lifted).

is short for input/output, and it's a popular domain among startups, engineers, programmers, and geeks. Technically it's the country code for the British Indian Ocean Territory, but it's also great if you want a super short domain."
If I got any of this wrong, my apologies. (
Mostly handed all this back to my teachers years ago.
Why does URL setup matter for SEO? When you see
, you immediately know it's Google. When you see
, you immediately know it's the iT Help site. So if you're planning to buy your own domain, choose carefully — it affects search engine results.
On using English: I did some digging, including this
on whether local-language URLs work. The key points:

Can URLs use local non-English words?
For sites that target users outside of English-speaking regions, it's sometimes unclear if they can really use their own language for URLs, and if so, what about non-English characters?
Google search uses URLs primarily as a way to address a piece of content. We use URLs to crawl a page, which is when Googlebot goes to check the page and to use the pages content for our search results.
As long as URLs are valid and unique, that's fine.

In short: as long as the URLs are valid and unique, you're fine (I won't go as far as saying it's a plus). But if you actually use Chinese in a URL, you'll often hit a specific issue — the URL turns into garbled-looking encoded characters. Here's an example. The original was
So Chinese URLs have this small downside — when you share them with someone, the URL is unreadable. And unreadable URLs tend to hurt SEO.
"Don't nest too deeply" — if your URL is something like
, that's not great for SEO either. Personally I think four levels is the sweet spot. My own blog maxes out at four:
"Descriptive words or keywords" — just make it so people can tell at a glance what page they're looking at. For example, "
" obviously refers to the JavaScript tag page.
